--- Page 1 ---
A revision of Litvak‚Äôs conjecture on Gaussian minima
and a volumetric zone conjecture
Dmitriy Kunisky
Department of Applied Mathematics & Statistics, Johns Hopkins University
May 3, 2026
Abstract
Litvak (2018) conjectured that, for any p > 0, the quantity E[minn
i=1 |gi|p] where
g ‚àºN(0, Œ£) is a centered Gaussian random vector is minimized among n√ón correlation
matrices Œ£ by the Gram matrix of the regular simplex in Rn‚àí1.
We disprove this
conjecture: the matrix with entries Œ£cos
ij
= cos(œÄ(i ‚àíj)/n) already achieves a smaller
moment for p = 2 and n = 4. We propose that Œ£cos is in fact the correct minimizer
of these moments for all p > 0 and n ‚â•1. Towards proving this, we conjecture a
volumetric extension of Fejes T¬¥oth‚Äôs zone conjecture (1973), whose covering version
was proved by Jiang and Polyanskii (2017). Conditional on this conjecture, we show
the stronger result that minn
i=1 |gi| for g ‚àºN(0, Œ£cos) is stochastically dominated by
minn
i=1 |hi| for h ‚àºN(0, Œ£) for any n√ón correlation matrix Œ£. Our counterexample Œ£cos
was found by the AlphaEvolve AI-assisted optimization system, and we also include a
brief discussion of its application to such problems.
1
Introduction
Let z ‚àºN(0, In) be a standard Gaussian vector. For an n √ó n correlation matrix Œ£ (i.e.,
one having Œ£ ‚™∞0 and Œ£ii = 1 for each i = 1, . . . , n), define the random variable
M(Œ£) :=
n
min
i=1 |(Œ£1/2z)i|.
Equivalently, if g ‚àºN(0, Œ£), then M(Œ£) has the same law as minn
i=1 |gi|. The goal of this
note is to consider, in various senses, what correlation matrix Œ£ makes the random variable
M(Œ£) as typically small as possible.
One natural choice, motivated by the solution (proven or conjectural) to many other such
extremal questions (see below for citations), is the covariance given by the Gram matrix of
a regular simplex in Rn‚àí1,
Œ£‚àÜ
ij :=
(
1
if i = j,
‚àí
1
n‚àí1
if i Ã∏= j.
1
arXiv:2605.02023v1  [math.PR]  3 May 2026


--- Page 2 ---
The following conjecture claiming a specific sense in which this is true has, to the best of
our knowledge, remained open since its proposal.
Conjecture 1.1 (Conjecture 5.1 of [Lit18]). Let n ‚â•3. For every n √ó n correlation matrix
Œ£ and p > 0,
E[M(Œ£‚àÜ)p] ‚â§E[M(Œ£)p].
The conjecture is a natural companion to the better-known simplex mean width con-
jecture. That problem asks whether the regular simplex maximizes the mean width among
simplices inscribed in the Euclidean ball. In Gaussian language, it asks whether the simplex
covariance maximizes among correlation matrices the expectation of the largest coordinate
of a Gaussian vector, E[maxn
i=1 gi] in the above notation. The same note [Lit18] also dis-
cusses many similar problems, and formulates the similar Conjecture 1.1 motivated in part
by considerations originating with a conjecture of Mallat‚ÄìZeitouni [MZ11].
The goal of the present note is to disprove this conjecture and propose a modification
that we believe to be correct. Our counterexample to Conjecture 1.1 will use the covariance
matrix
Œ£cos
ij := cos
œÄ(i ‚àíj)
n

for 1 ‚â§i, j ‚â§n,
a rank-two correlation matrix, which already ‚Äúbeats‚Äù Œ£‚àÜin the context of Conjecture 1.1
for n = 4 and p = 2. We visualize the matrices Œ£cos and Œ£‚àÜin Figure 1.
Organization
The rest of the note is organized as follows.
First, we prove that the
above is indeed a counterexample to Conjecture 1.1 (Theorem 2.2). Second, we propose
a strengthened version of Conjecture 1.1 with Œ£‚àÜreplaced by Œ£cos (Conjecture 3.1). Third,
we propose an ancillary geometric conjecture (Conjecture 4.1) which implies our conjec-
ture (Theorem 4.2). Finally, we give a brief discussion of the helpful and perhaps instructive
role of artificial intelligence assistants and black-box optimization in our discovery of these
results (Section 5.2).
Notation
We write N(¬µ, Œ£) for the multivariate Gaussian measure with mean ¬µ and
covariance Œ£. We write Sn‚àí1(r) ‚äÇRn for the sphere of radius r in Rn, and Sn‚àí1 := Sn‚àí1(1)
for the unit sphere.
2
Counterexample to Conjecture 1.1
We first discuss the structure of Œ£cos and M(Œ£cos). Define the vectors
vj :=

cos
(j ‚àí1)œÄ
n

, sin
(j ‚àí1)œÄ
n

‚ààR2 for 1 ‚â§j ‚â§n.
These define lines whose intersection points with the unit circle in R2 are the vertices of
a regular 2n-gon. Œ£cos is the Gram matrix of these vectors vj, Œ£cos
ij
= ‚ü®vi, vj‚ü©. Thus, if
z ‚àºN(0, I2) and we define
hk := ‚ü®z, vk‚ü©,

Figure 1: The cosine covariance and the simplex covariance for n = 16, with entries shown
from ‚àí1 in white to 1 in black.
then h has the law N(0, Œ£cos). The law of the associated minimum random variable therefore
can be realized as
M(Œ£cos)
(d)
= min
1‚â§j‚â§n |‚ü®z, vj‚ü©|.
Proposition 2.1 (Law of M(Œ£cos)). Let r2 ‚àºœá2(2) and Œ∏ ‚àºUnif([0, œÄ
2n]) be independent.1
Then, M(Œ£cos) has the same law as r sin(Œ∏). Consequently, for all t, p > 0 we have
P[M(Œ£cos) ‚â•t] = 2n
œÄ
Z
œÄ
2n
0
exp

‚àí
t2
2 sin2 Œ∏

dŒ∏,
E[M(Œ£cos)p] = 2p/2Œì

1 + p
2
 2n
œÄ
Z
œÄ
2n
0
sinp Œ∏ dŒ∏.
Proof. Let z ‚àºN(0, I2). Write this vector as z = (r cos œï, r sin œï) in polar coordinates. We
have r2 = z2
1 +z2
2 has law œá2(2), œï is uniformly distributed with law Unif([0, 2œÄ]), and the two
are independent by a standard property of Gaussian random vectors. Since absolute values
identify angles modulo œÄ, the n lines perpendicular to the vk divide the upper semicircle
of the unit circle in R2 into n equal arcs. The angular distance Œ∏ from œï to nearest such
perpendicular line therefore has law Unif([0, œÄ
2n]). By construction, we then have
M(Œ£cos)
(d)
= min
k |‚ü®z, vk‚ü©| = r sin(Œ∏).
The tail and moment formulas follow from the independence of r and Œ∏ and explicit formulas
for their densities.
Theorem 2.2 (Counterexample to Conjecture 1.1). For n = 4, we have
E[M(Œ£cos)2] < E[M(Œ£‚àÜ)2].
Thus Conjecture 1.1 is false for n = 4 and p = 2.
1The law of such r is also sometimes called the Rayleigh distribution.
3


--- Page 4 ---
Proof. This is simple to verify by a numerical computation, but let us give a careful rigorous
justification for the sake of completeness. By Proposition 2.1, we may compute exactly
E[M(Œ£cos)2] = 1 ‚àín sin(œÄ/n)
œÄ
.
For n = 4, this is
1 ‚àí2
‚àö
2
œÄ
‚àà[0.099683, 0.099684].
It remains to bound the same expectation for the simplex covariance from below. For
n = 4, realize the simplex covariance as the Gram matrix Œ£‚àÜ
ij = ‚ü®ui, uj‚ü©for
u1 = (1, 1, 1)
‚àö
3
,
u2 = (1, ‚àí1, ‚àí1)
‚àö
3
,
u3 = (‚àí1, 1, ‚àí1)
‚àö
3
,
u4 = (‚àí1, ‚àí1, 1)
‚àö
3
.
Let g ‚àºN(0, I3) and write g = (rw1, rw2, rw3) in spherical coordinates in R3. Then r and
w are independent, E[r2] = 3, and
M(Œ£‚àÜ) = r min
i |‚ü®w, ui‚ü©|.
By the sign-change and coordinate-permutation symmetries of the set of lines spanned by
the ui, it suffices to integrate over the sector w1 ‚â•w2 ‚â•w3 ‚â•0 of the sphere S2 = {w ‚àà
R3 : w2
1 + w2
2 + w2
3 = 1}. On this sector,
min
i |‚ü®(w1, w2, w3), ui‚ü©| = |w1 ‚àíw2 ‚àíw3|
‚àö
3
.
Using the chart for this sector
(w1, w2, w3) =
(1, s, st)
‚àö
1 + s2 + s2t2,
s, t ‚àà[0, 1]
and multiplying the integral over the sector by the 23 ¬∑ 3! = 48 congruent sectors, we get the
concrete integral expression
E[M(Œ£‚àÜ)2] = 12
œÄ
Z 1
0
Z 1
0
s(1 ‚àís ‚àíst)2
(1 + s2 + s2t2)5/2 ds dt.
Subdividing the unit square into 400 √ó 400 boxes and computing interval arithmetic bounds
on this integral numerically gives
E[M(Œ£‚àÜ)2] ‚àà[0.139622, 0.144779].
This lower bound is larger than 1 ‚àí2
‚àö
2/œÄ, completing the proof.

Figure 2: Upper tail probabilities of M(Œ£) for the cosine covariance, the simplex covariance,
the identity covariance, 50 random full-rank covariances, and 50 random rank-two covariances
with n = 8. Probabilities for random and simplex covariances are Monte Carlo estimates.
3
Strengthened revision of Conjecture 1.1
We further believe that the cosine covariance is the true optimizer in Conjecture 1.1, and
that this holds in a stronger sense than moment comparison.
We propose the following
revision of the Conjecture:
Conjecture 3.1. For every n ‚â•1, every correlation matrix Œ£, and every t ‚â•0,
P[M(Œ£cos) ‚â•t] ‚â§P[M(Œ£) ‚â•t].
That is, M(Œ£cos) is stochastically dominated by M(Œ£). In particular,
E[M(Œ£cos)p] ‚â§E[M(Œ£)p]
for every p > 0.
This phenomenon is illustrated in Figure 2, which plots the tail probabilities in question
for covariances Œ£cos, Œ£‚àÜ, the identity matrix, and many independent draws of random full-
and low-rank correlation matrices (formed as Gram matrices of independent unit vectors
of suitable dimension). We note that the tail probability function of Œ£cos seems to be an
‚Äúoutlier‚Äù in the space of correlation matrices, with exceptional decay much faster than either
of the other fixed examples and than typical random covariances.
5


--- Page 6 ---
4
Volumetric zone conjecture
We have not been able to prove Conjecture 3.1. However, we can reduce it to the following
geometric conjecture in the spirit of the Fejes T¬¥oth zone conjecture [T¬¥ot73]‚Äînow the Jiang‚Äì
Polyanskii theorem [JP17]‚Äîand other similar results. See Section 5.1 for more references.
Conjecture 4.1 (Volumetric zone conjecture). Let d ‚â•2, let u1, . . . , un ‚ààSd‚àí1, and let
Œ± ‚àà[0, œÄ/2]. For a unit vector u, define the spherical zone
Z(u, Œ±) := {v ‚ààSd‚àí1 : |‚ü®v, u‚ü©| ‚â§sin(Œ±)}.
For j = 1, . . . , n, define
vj :=

cos
(j ‚àí1)œÄ
n

, sin
(j ‚àí1)œÄ
n

, 0, . . . , 0

‚ààSd‚àí1.
Then
œÉd‚àí1
 n[
j=1
Z(uj, Œ±)
!
‚â§œÉd‚àí1
 n[
j=1
Z(vj, Œ±)
!
,
where œÉd‚àí1 denotes the surface measure on Sd‚àí1 normalized to be a probability measure.
The conjecture says that among n equal-width zones on the sphere, the zones around n
evenly-spaced great subspheres sharing a subsphere of codimension two maximize the surface
measure of the union. The original zone conjecture of [T¬¥ot73], proved by [JP17], asked for
the minimum width 2Œ± required for the union of n spherical zones to cover the entire sphere,
claiming that the minimum is achieved by the same configuration.
Our conjecture may
be viewed as generalizing this to ‚Äúthinner‚Äù configurations of zones that only cover a given
fraction of the sphere. We visualize the conjectural optimal configuration in Figure 3.
This conjecture implies our conjecture on Gaussian minima:
Theorem 4.2. Conjecture 4.1 implies Conjecture 3.1.
Proof. The cases n = 1 and t = 0 of Conjecture 3.1 are trivial, so suppose n ‚â•2 and t > 0.
Suppose Conjecture 4.1 holds. Choose unit vectors u1, . . . , un in Rn whose Gram matrix is
Œ£, i.e., having Œ£ij = ‚ü®ui, uj‚ü©. Let g ‚àºN(0, In). Then
(‚ü®g, u1‚ü©, . . . , ‚ü®g, un‚ü©) ‚àºN(0, Œ£),
so
M(Œ£)
(d)
=
n
min
j=1 |‚ü®g, uj‚ü©|.
For t > 0, define the union of slabs
B(t) :=
n[
j=1
{v ‚ààRn : |‚ü®v, uj‚ü©| ‚â§t}.
Then
P[M(Œ£) ‚â§t] = Œ≥n(B(t)),
6


--- Page 7 ---
Figure 3: The optimal configuration of spherical zones maximizing the surface measure of
their union proposed in Conjecture 4.1.
where Œ≥n = N(0, In) is the standard Gaussian measure on Rn.
Write S(t, r) := B(t) ‚à©Sn‚àí1(r), the intersection of this union of slabs with the sphere of
radius r. When t/r ‚â•1, S(t, r) = Sn‚àí1(r). When 0 < t/r < 1, we have by definition
S(t, r) = r ¬∑
n[
j=1
Z

uj, arcsin
 t
r

.
Thus, defining
Œ±(t, r) :=
(
œÄ/2
if r ‚â§t,
arcsin(t/r)
if r > t
we compute
P[M(Œ£) ‚â§t] = Œ≥n(B(t))
=
P
g‚àºN(0,In)[g ‚ààB(t)]
=
P
g‚àºN(0,In)[g ‚ààS(t, ‚à•g‚à•)]
=
P
g‚àºN(0,In)
"
g
‚à•g‚à•‚àà
n[
j=1
Z (uj, Œ±(t, ‚à•g‚à•))
#
and since ‚à•g‚à•is independent of g/‚à•g‚à•and the latter has law œÉn‚àí1, we have
=
E
g‚àºN(0,In)
"
œÉn‚àí1
 n[
j=1
Z (uj, Œ±(t, ‚à•g‚à•))
!#
7


--- Page 8 ---
and letting vi be as in Conjecture 4.1 and using that we assume the conjecture holds,
‚â§
E
g‚àºN(0,In)
"
œÉn‚àí1
 n[
j=1
Z (vj, Œ±(t, ‚à•g‚à•))
!#
and reversing the same manipulations
= P[M(Œ£cos) ‚â§t],
giving the result since the law of M(Œ£) does not have atoms for any correlation matrix Œ£.
The result on moments follows by the standard manipulation of expressing the expectations
as integrals over the tail probabilities.
5
Discussion
5.1
Related work
Conjecture 1.1 appears as Conjecture 5.1 in Litvak‚Äôs survey [Lit18] on the simplex mean
width conjecture (which is Conjecture 1.4 of the survey). Litvak presents it as part of a
family of Gaussian extremal problems related to simplex covariances, including the mean-
width problem, the simplex code conjecture, and various related geometric claims.
One motivation for Conjecture 1.1 comes from a conjecture of Mallat and Zeitouni on
the Karhunen‚ÄìLo`eve basis in nonlinear reconstruction [MZ11]. Litvak notes that the orig-
inal intuition behind that problem would have followed from a stronger statement about
minima with p = 2 for which the identity covariance was proposed as optimal. The same
discussion records that van Handel gave an example showing that, in the three-variable case,
the simplex covariance improves on the identity covariance. While the original conjecture of
Mallat‚ÄìZeitouni remains open, it was later resolved by Litvak and Tikhomirov up to absolute
constants [LT18].
The estimates of Gordon, Litvak, Sch¬®utt, and Werner on minima of Gaussian and more
general random variables give another nearby point of comparison [GLSW05, GLSW06].
Those works give sharp-order bounds for minima and order statistics under broad assump-
tions. The present problem is different in that the marginal variances are fixed and the
covariance matrix itself is the object to optimize.
The geometric input proposed here is a volumetric variant of Fejes T¬¥oth‚Äôs zone prob-
lem [T¬¥ot73].
Jiang and Polyanskii proved the covering version of Fejes T¬¥oth‚Äôs conjec-
ture in all dimensions [JP17]. Ortega-Moreno gave a different proof for the equal-width
case [OM21], and Zhao later streamlined that argument [Zha22]. Glazyrin, Karasev, and
Polyanskii developed polynomial plank methods which also give a strengthening for spher-
ical segments [GKP23]. For a broader view of plank problems and their applications, see
Verreault‚Äôs survey [Ver22]. While Conjecture 4.1 seems like a natural generalization, to the
best of our knowledge it has not appeared in the literature.
8


--- Page 9 ---
5.2
AI assistance
Our Œ£cos counterexample was found by black-box minimization of the functions fp(Œ£) =
E[M(Œ£)p] using AlphaEvolve [NVE+25], an optimization system based on code generation
with large language models (see, e.g., [NRT25, NRT26] for other recent results obtained
using it). After AlphaEvolve surfaced Œ£cos it was easy to recognize (for instance from a plot
like in Figure 1) that there was an underlying continuous function, and then to identify it
as the cosine. Experimenting with minimizing gt(Œ£) = P[M(Œ£) ‚â•t] led to the stronger
formulation of Conjecture 3.1.
Afterwards, we also considered whether more conventional optimization methods such as
differential evolution and BFGS-type gradient methods could find the same optimizer. While
these often gave improvements on the simplex covariance that would just as well disprove
Conjecture 1.1, they only rarely converged to (what we believe is) the true minimizer Œ£cos,
especially for n larger than the dimension of the smallest counterexample n = 4 (for n = 3,
M(Œ£cos) and M(Œ£‚àÜ) have the same law up to a coordinate sign change) and especially when
optimizing the gt functions which we evaluate by estimating the probabilities empirically.2
We also found that both the fp and gt functions can have spurious local minimizers different
than the global minimizer Œ£cos. Thus, aside from convenience of implementation, AlphaE-
volve seems to offer an advantage as a research tool in this setting. In general, inspecting
the output of AlphaEvolve or similar systems by hand to look for tractable structure seems
to be a useful further step to translate its results into mathematical insights. It is an intrigu-
ing question whether this process itself could be automated to further accelerate research
assisted with black-box optimization.
We also remark that the connection with the Fejes T¬¥oth zone conjecture and related
literature was unknown to us until it was surfaced by GPT-5.5 Pro when asked to prove
Conjecture 3.1. On the other hand, this model repeatedly produced proofs which used that
Conjecture as if it were included in the results of Fejes T¬¥oth or others cited above. Its
mistake was nonetheless informative: once the unsupported step was isolated, it pointed
directly to the reasonable and independently interesting Conjecture 4.1.
Acknowledgments
Thanks to the Google DeepMind AlphaEvolve team for providing early access to AlphaEvolve
and in particular to Adam Zsolt Wagner for answering my many questions about using the
system.
References
[GKP23]
Alexey Glazyrin, Roman Karasev, and Alexandr Polyanskii. Covering by planks
and avoiding zeros of polynomials. International Mathematics Research Notices,
2023(13):11684‚Äì11700, 2023.
2When run over only rank-two correlation matrices, these simpler methods did often find Œ£cos; however,
this is a rather unrealistic point of comparison since we did not know before seeing the result of AlphaEvolve
that the optimizer should be of such low rank.
